      <title>More than 200 killed in coltan mine collapse in eastern DRC, officials say</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/more-than-200-killed-in-coltan-mine-collapse-in-eastern-drc-officials-say</link>
      <description><p>Rubaya mine produces about 15% of the world’s coltan, which is processed into tantalum, used in mobile phones</p><p>More than 200 people were killed this week in a collapse at the Rubaya coltan mine in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, Lumumba Kambere Muyisa, a spokesperson for the rebel-appointed governor of the province where the mine is located, told Reuters on Friday.</p><p>Rubaya produces about 15% of the world’s coltan, which is processed into tantalum – a heat-resistant metal that is in high demand by makers of mobile phones, computers, aerospace components and gas turbines. The site, where local people dig manually for a few dollars a day, has been under the control of the M23 rebel group since 2024.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/more-than-200-killed-in-coltan-mine-collapse-in-eastern-drc-officials-say">Continue reading...</a></description>

    <item>
      <title>Islamic State claims attack on international airport and airbase in Niger</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/niger-airport-airbase-attack-islamic-state</link>
      <description><p>Motorcycle-riding militants launch strikes using heavy weaponry and drones, damaging planes belonging to Ivorian carrier and Togolese airline</p><p>Islamic State in the Sahel has claimed responsibility for <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/29/gunfire-blasts-heard-airport-niamey-niger-planes-hit-reports">an audacious assault at the international airport</a> and adjacent air force base in Niamey, the capital of Niger, according to the SITE Intelligence Group, which tracks jihadist activity and communications worldwide.</p><p>The attack, which began shortly after midnight on Thursday, reportedly involved motorcycle-riding militants who launched a “surprise and coordinated” strike using heavy weaponry and drones, according to statements released via IS in the Sahel’s propaganda arm, Amaq news agency.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/niger-airport-airbase-attack-islamic-state">Continue reading...</a></description>

    <item>
      <title>South Africa expels top Israeli diplomat over ‘insulting attacks’ on president</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/south-africa-expels-israel-diplomat-insults-president</link>
      <description><p>Ariel Seidman declared persona non grata and given 72 hours to leave country after remarks on social media</p><p>South Africa and Israel have engaged in a tit-for-tat expulsion of senior diplomats, after South Africa ordered Israel’s chargé d’affaires to leave within 72 hours, citing “insulting attacks” on South Africa’s president, Cyril Ramaphosa, on social media.</p><p>Ariel Seidman, the chargé d’affaires at Israel’s embassy in Pretoria, was declared persona non grata by South Africa’s department for international relations and cooperation (DIRCO) in a <a href="https://dirco.gov.za/israeli-charge-daffaires-declared-persona-non-grata/">statement on its website</a> on Friday afternoon.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/south-africa-expels-israel-diplomat-insults-president">Continue reading...</a></description>

    <item>
      <title>Niger’s military ruler vows retaliation after gunfire and explosions heard in capital</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/29/gunfire-blasts-heard-airport-niamey-niger-planes-hit-reports</link>
      <description><p>General Tchiani accuses France, Benin and the Ivory Coast of links to attack near Niamey’s airport and thanks Russian troops for defence efforts </p><p> </p><p>Heavy security has been deployed around the main airport in Niger’s capital, Niamey, after overnight gunfire and explosions that the country’s military ruler blamed without evidence on France, Benin and Côte d’Ivoire.</p><p>The shooting and detonations began shortly after midnight on Wednesday, according to residents of a neighbourhood near the airport, which is next to Base Aérienne 101, a military base previously used by <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/article/2024/may/03/us-confirms-russian-forces-have-entered-airbase-in-niger-where-us-troops-are-stationed">US and then Russian troops</a>.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/29/gunfire-blasts-heard-airport-niamey-niger-planes-hit-reports">Continue reading...</a></description>

    <item>
      <title>Hundreds feared dead in attempt to cross Mediterranean during cyclone</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/26/hundreds-feared-dead-after-attempting-to-cross-mediterranean-amid-cyclone-harry</link>
      <description><p>Fifty killed in one incident as Italian authorities estimate 380 people may have drowned last week</p><p>Up to 380 people may have drowned attempting to cross the Mediterranean last week as Cyclone Harry battered southern Italy and Malta, the Italian coastguard has said, as a shipwreck with the loss of 50 lives was confirmed by Maltese authorities.</p><p>Just one person, who was hospitalised in Malta, survived the shipwreck, which happened on Friday.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/26/hundreds-feared-dead-after-attempting-to-cross-mediterranean-amid-cyclone-harry">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <title>Costa Rica heads to polls amid fears of authoritarian turn</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/feb/01/costa-rica-polls-fears-authoritarian-turn-laura-fernandez</link>
      <description><p>Voters to choose president and 57 members of congress, with current president’s hardline pick Laura Fernández expected to win first round</p><p>Costa Rica heads to the polls on Sunday in an election dominated by increasing insecurity and warnings of an authoritarian turn in a country long seen as a model of liberal democracy in the region.</p><p>Crime is a big concern for many voters as criminal groups battle to control lucrative cocaine trafficking routes to Europe and the US, casting a shadow on the Central American country famous for its wildlife tourism.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/feb/01/costa-rica-polls-fears-authoritarian-turn-laura-fernandez">Continue reading...</a></description>

    <item>
      <title>Mexico president says Trump tariffs on Cuba’s oil suppliers could trigger humanitarian crisis</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/mexico-sheinbaum-trump-tariffs-cuba-oil-crisis</link>
      <description><p>Island country only has oil enough to last 15-20 days, and 12-hour blackouts have become commonplace</p><p><a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/mexico">Mexico</a>’s president, <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/claudia-sheinbaum">Claudia Sheinbaum</a>, has warned that <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/donaldtrump">Donald Trump</a>’s move to slap new tariffs on countries sending oil to <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/cuba">Cuba</a> could trigger a humanitarian crisis on the island, which is already suffering from chronic fuel shortages and regular blackouts.</p><p>The US president signed an executive order on Thursday declaring a national emergency and laying the groundwork for such tariffs, ratcheting up the pressure to topple the communist government in Havana.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/mexico-sheinbaum-trump-tariffs-cuba-oil-crisis">Continue reading...</a></description>

    <item>
      <title>Panama supreme court cancels Hong Kong company’s canal contracts</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/court-ends-hong-kong-china-panama-canal-contracts</link>
      <description><p>Panama’s president says strategic waterway will operate as normal after ruling that advances US policy aims</p><p>Panama’s president said ports at each end of the Panama canal would operate as usual after the country’s supreme court ruled the concession held by a subsidiary of a Chinese company was unconstitutional.</p><p>The court’s decision on Thursday, which helps US attempts to block any Chinese influence over the strategic waterway, immediately drew a sharp rebuke from Beijing.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/court-ends-hong-kong-china-panama-canal-contracts">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <description><p>Law will give private companies more control but experts unsure whether changes go far enough for US</p><p>Venezuela’s acting president has signed into law a bill making significant changes to the country’s oil sector after pressure from the US to open it up to foreign private investment.</p><p>The new hydrocarbons law promises to give private companies control over oil production and sales, ease taxes and allow for independent arbitration of disputes, while largely maintaining state control over oil production.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/venezuela-approves-bill-to-open-oil-sector-to-foreign-investment-after-us-pressure">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <description><p>Millions told to stay home in US and more than a million are left without power, while Australia faces record heatwave</p><p>Cold weather across a vast swathe of the eastern US has been the likely cause of at least 49 deaths in the past week.</p><p>At one point, about 213 million people were under some sort of winter weather warnings, affecting areas from New Mexico to New England – a spread of about 2,000 miles (3,200km).       <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/feb/01/living-hell-of-north-koreas-paradise-on-earth-scheme-back-in-spotlight-in-japan</link>
      <description><p>Plaintiffs in case say they were lured from Japan, exploited for labour and cut off from families for generations</p><p>It has been more than six decades since Eiko Kawasaki left Japan to begin a new life in <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/north-korea">North Korea</a>. Then 17, she was among tens of thousands of people with Korean heritage who had been lured to the communist state by the promise of a “paradise on Earth”.</p><p>Instead, they encountered something closer to a living hell. They were denied basic human rights and forced to endure extreme hardship. Official promises of free education and healthcare plus guaranteed jobs and housing had been a cruel mirage. And to their horror, they were prevented from travelling to Japan to visit the families they had left behind.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/feb/01/living-hell-of-north-koreas-paradise-on-earth-scheme-back-in-spotlight-in-japan">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <description><p>Prime minister suggests US president was ‘talking more about Canada’ when asked for reaction to Beijing visit</p><p>Prominent Hong Kong and Uyghur activists living in exile in the UK have accused Starmer of seeking China’s desperate approval, after the prime minister visited Beijing for the first time in eight years this week.</p><p>Pro-democracy campaigner and prominent critic of the Communist Party, Finn Lau said the Hong Kong community is disappointed by Starmer’s visit, but unsurprised by the government’s “short sightedness”.</p><p>“While British citizen Jimmy Lai remains imprisoned and Uyghurs continue to suffer atrocity crimes, we take no comfort in this decision and will not be silenced.</p><p>We look forward to receiving urgent assurances from the government regarding those who were placed under sanction together with us, and take this opportunity to express our ongoing solidarity with the Uyghur people, whose cause we will not drop.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/politics/live/2026/jan/30/donald-trump-keir-starmer-china-uk-us-trade-deals-tariffs-latest-news">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <title>‘Keep on dreaming’: could Europe really defend itself without the US?</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/31/european-defence-disarray-future-nato-trump</link>
      <description><p>Nato chief has glibly dismissed prospect of coping without US support, but in the age of Trump the case for autonomy is growing</p><p>The Nato secretary general, Mark Rutte, was typically blunt when he met members of the European parliament this week. From the dais of the blond-wood committee room in Brussels, he was clear: “If anyone thinks that the European Union, or Europe as a whole, can defend itself without the US, keep on dreaming. You can’t. We can’t.”</p><p>And if Europe wanted to supplant the US nuclear deterrent, existing spending commitments would have to double, he added – “so hey, good luck!”</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/31/european-defence-disarray-future-nato-trump">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <description><p>Thousands set to gather in Budapest after János Lázár’s remarks captured on video</p><p>Thousands of people are set to gather in Budapest to demand the resignation of a senior Hungarian politician, for making a racist remark against Roma people earlier this month.</p><p>János Lázár told attendees at a political forum that migration was not the solution to the country’s labour shortage. “Since there are no migrants, and someone has to clean the bathrooms on the InterCity trains,” Lázár said Roma people would do the job, using an offensive slur in his speech.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/31/protest-janos-lazar-hungary-politician-anti-roma">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2026/jan/31/uk-and-eu-to-explore-renewed-talks-on-defence-cooperation</link>
      <description><p>Keir Starmer says he wants to ‘go further’ in relations with Brussels as ministers look to restart stalled negotiations</p><p>The UK and the EU are exploring the prospect of new talks on closer defence cooperation, as Keir Starmer stressed on Friday that he wanted to “go further” in the UK’s relationship with Brussels.<br><br>
 Maroš Šefčovič, the EU’s trade commissioner, is due in London for talks next week, with trade, energy and fisheries on the agenda. But diplomatic sources said the UK is keen to discuss restarting negotiations on defence as soon as it can.</p><p>Talks for the UK to join the EU’s €150bn (£130bn) Security Action for Europe (Safe) defence fund collapsed in November 2025 amid claims that the EU had set too high a price on entry to the programme.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2026/jan/31/uk-and-eu-to-explore-renewed-talks-on-defence-cooperation">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <description><p>Pressure mounts after government said it would publish names of those killed during recent unrest</p><p>Calls are growing inside Iran for an independent inquiry into the number of people killed during recent protests after the government said it would oversee the publication of the names of the deceased.</p><p>The highly unusual government move, announced on Thursday, is designed to head off claims that crimes against humanity have been committed and that as many as 30,000 Iranians have been killed.       <description><p>Nick Carter says easing controls on MDMA will allow drug to be used as alternative treatment for those with PTSD</p><p>A former head of the British military is calling for the government to ease restrictions on the party drug MDMA so that it can be tested more cheaply as a treatment for veterans with post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD).</p><p>Sir Nick Carter, who was chief of the defence staff until 2021, said existing regulations meant a single gram of “medical grade” MDMA cost about £10,000 compared with a street price of about £40, inflating the cost of trials.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2026/feb/01/british-army-chief-government-drugs-mdma-therapy-veterans">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/feb/01/inside-myanmar-armed-resistance-junta-coup-photo-essay</link>
      <description><p>Five years after the junta’s coup, the civil war devastating Myanmar has reached a turning point. The military is carrying out large-scale counter-offensives across the country to reclaim territory seized by pro-democracy rebels of various ethnic and religious backgrounds</p><p>In Tanintharyi, the southernmost region of Myanmar, the local resistance has managed to contain the military. After five years of guerrilla warfare, the revolutionary youth there remain determined to restore democracy through armed struggle.</p><p>A long, narrow stretch of land at the southern tip of Myanmar, between the Andaman Sea to the west and Thailand to the east, Tanintharyi region is one of the areas where the resistance challenges the military’s authority. For decades, the region has been home to an armed rebellion led by the Karen ethnic minority, which operated mainly in the peripheral mountains.</p><p>Soldiers from the Karen National Union (KNU) inspect the ruins of a Buddhist monastery destroyed by a junta airstrike in Myeik district, Tanintharyi region</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/feb/01/inside-myanmar-armed-resistance-junta-coup-photo-essay">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/junta-backed-party-victory-myanmar-sham-election</link>
      <description><p>Human rights groups and some western countries have denounced the election, the first held since the 2021 coup, describing it as neither free nor fair</p><p>Myanmar’s military-backed party has completed a sweeping victory in the country’s <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2025/dec/24/myanmars-first-election-since-the-2021-coup-everything-you-need-to-know">three-phase general election</a>, state media said, cementing an <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/25/myanmar-election-enters-final-stage-amid-airstrikes-and-exclusions">outcome long expected</a> after a tightly controlled political process held during civil war and widespread repression.</p><p>The <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/03/myanmar-election-first-phase-election-result">Union and Solidarity Party (USDP</a>) dominated all phases of the vote, winning an overwhelming majority in the two legislative chambers in <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/myanmar">Myanmar</a>. It secured 232 of the 263 seats up for grabs in the lower Pyithu Hluttaw house and 109 of the 157 seats announced so far in the Amyotha Hluttaw upper chamber, according to results released on Thursday and Friday.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/world/2026/jan/30/junta-backed-party-victory-myanmar-sham-election">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <description><p>Woman claims she spent night at Royal Lodge and had tour of Buckingham Palace, says lawyer</p><p>A second woman has alleged that <a href="https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/clymxxlm3xzo.amp">Jeffrey Epstein</a> sent her to the UK to have a sexual encounter with Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor, according to her lawyer.</p><p>The woman has claimed she spent the night at the former prince’s residence Royal Lodge in 2010, her US lawyer, Brad Edwards, <a href="https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/clymxxlm3xzo.amp">told the BBC</a>. The woman, who is not British, was in her 20s at the time, it was reported.</p><p><em>In the UK and Ireland, <a href="https://www.samaritans.org/">Samaritans</a> can be contacted on freephone 116 123, or email <a href="mailto:jo@samaritans.org">jo@samaritans.org</a> or <a href="mailto:jo@samaritans.ie">jo@samaritans.ie</a>. In the US, you can call or text the <a href="https://988lifeline.org/">National Suicide Prevention Lifeline</a> on 988, chat on <a href="https://988lifeline.org/chat/">988lifeline.org</a>, or <a href="https://www.crisistextline.org/">text HOME</a> to 741741 to connect with a crisis counselor. In Australia, the crisis support service <a href="https://www.lifeline.org.au/">Lifeline</a> is 13 11 14. Other international helplines can be found at <a href="http://www.befrienders.org/">befrienders.org</a></em></p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/uk-news/2026/feb/01/second-woman-alleges-epstein-sent-her-to-uk-to-have-sex-with-andrew-mountbatten-windsor">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <description><p>This blog is now closed. Find more of our coverage on Jeffery Epstein <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/jeffrey-epstein">here</a></p><ul><li><p><a href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/jan/31/epstein-files-what-have-we-learned-prince-andrew-peter-mandelson-elon-musk-donald-trump">What have we learned from the newly released Epstein files?</a></p></li></ul><p><a href="https://www.justice.gov/epstein/files/DataSet%209/EFTA01247451.pdf">According to one file</a>, <strong>Mountbatten-Windsor</strong> was said to be <strong>“very focused” on financier Harlan Peltz’s girlfriend during a dinner with Maxwell.</strong></p><p>The apparent FBI document details a 2020 interview with Peltz in which he provided information to agents about Maxwell.</p><p>Peltz was at a dinner with Maxwell and Prince Andrew and Peltz’s then girlfriend. <strong>Prince Andrew was very focused on Peltz’s girlfriend.</strong> Maxwell would sometimes mention Prince Andrew’s name and that they were friends.</p><p>Maxwell would have outrageous parties back then. She liked to put people in uncomfortable positions for her entertainment. Peltz realised that he was a pawn to her and she would try to use him. Sometime later on he found out that he was listed in Epstein’s black book.</p><p>People in the finance world never seemed to know how Epstein got his money.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/live/2026/jan/31/jeffrey-epstein-files-latest-news-updates-prince-andrew-elon-musk-peter-mandelson-donald-trump">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <title>Why TikTok’s first week of American ownership was a disaster</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2026/feb/01/tiktok-first-week</link>
      <description><p>App endured a major outage and user backlash over perceived censorship. Now it’s facing an inquiry by the California governor and an ascendant competitor</p><p>A little more than one week ago, TikTok stepped on to US shores as a naturalized citizen. Ever since, the video app has been fighting for its life.</p><p>TikTok’s calamitous emigration began on 22 January when its Chinese parent company, ByteDance, <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/jan/22/tiktok-us-venture-oracle">finalized</a> a deal to sell the app to a group of US investors, among them the business software giant Oracle. The app’s time under Chinese ownership had been marked by a meteoric ascent to more than a billion users, which left incumbents such as Instagram looking like the next Myspace. But TikTok’s short new life in the US has been less than auspicious.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2026/feb/01/tiktok-first-week">Continue reading...</a></description>

      <title>Chicago mayor orders police to investigate alleged illegal ICE activity in city</title>
      <link>https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/jan/31/chicago-ice-brandon-johnson</link>
      <description><p>Brandon Johnson gives police ‘clear procedure’ to follow if they witness or get reports of agents involved in illegal activity</p><p>Chicago’s mayor Brandon Johnson has ordered Chicago police to investigate and document alleged illegal activity by federal immigration (ICE) agents in the city, a move that will escalate tensions over jurisdiction between local and federal authorities.</p><p>The executive order, titled <a href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/mayor/press_room/press_releases/2026/january/ice-on-notice.html">ICE on Notice</a>, gives Chicago police “clear procedure” to follow if they witness or receive reports of ICE agents involved in illegal activity and refer evidence of potential violations to city prosecutors.</p> <a href="https://www.theguardian.com/us-news/2026/jan/31/chicago-ice-brandon-johnson">Continue reading...</a></description>
